speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five with Norbert Kerenyi. You were going to tell me about the commander.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I started to tell you that he was beginning to have his former comrades. And when he was arrested, the house with the leading professors sent a delegation to the Russian commander-- it was the Ukrainian army that occupied the west-- trying to get him out and explain how much he helped. And they went down and told-- the commander didn't see them, but to another officer who was wiling to see them-- that why they were there. To which he responded that they have exactly two minutes to leave. And if they are still there, then he's going to arrest them as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So their attempt to help him was totally unsuccessful. He was taken to a prisoner of war camp. They did not start any proceed-- proceedings against him. And he was in this prisoner of war camps, and somehow managed to let people know where he is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the Hungarian government came in and took over, some of the people who were hiding in this place were members of the illegal Communist party and they had contacts through the government and to-- through the government to the Russians. And after a year of being in this prisoner of war camp, they arranged to bring him back. And he was brought back and released in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Budapest. And he stayed there for awhile, and then he left for Vienna. And as the Communist system started to ease, he returned to Hungary again and, of all the professions he took up to become, he became a producer of nightclub shows. And at one of our visits to Hungary, we went to the nightclub show that he produced. It was a very good show. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Yeretzyan-Ara, George. George being George, the first name. And Yeretzyan-Ara is his family name. And we met him there, and we told him that we are-- and to out astonishment, we recognized him. He looked older, of course, than he was, but he was already around thirty at the time. But he said that he recognized us, which I think was a polite, white lie. But we talked to him, and he was comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how your life, then, proceeded after liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: After liberation, I felt very strongly that I really have nothing to do here, and I decided to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] join the Zionist movement. And I decided to-- to join the extreme left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hashomer Hatzair.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hashomer Hatzair. And we were the first quota of the Hashomer Hatzair after liberation. And I was active in this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] movement until they expelled me. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened because I managed to get into medical school. And according to the rules of this movement, you could not go to university because they did not agree to that. And since I was not willing to resign-- because I told them that they are wrong-- they eventually expelled me. But it was not really an unfriendly expulsion. We remained friends and we maintained contact. We are having contact even today. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A member of this quota became, subsequently, the leader of the [NON-ENGLISH] movement in Hungary, and-- for example, he asked me-- when they had to escape from Hungary-- he asked me to help, because they knew that I knew a way to get to the northern border. And I was already a member of the Pathology Institute as a medical student when they asked me. It was in March 1949. And this was an interesting episode because I knew the risks. I still felt that I must help them because they told me that their usual contact was arrested, and I would be the only one who would lead them to Czechoslovakia. Putnok, where my father was born and where he had a number of friends, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was very close to the border. And I took the [NON-ENGLISH] from [INAUDIBLE] through the Czech border. I left the Institute and we went on the express train to-- to [INAUDIBLE] where this [NON-ENGLISH] was with a young boy at the time. This young boy was around 15. And we had the list of the people who we had to take over, and we had the address. And I told him that we cannot keep this paper. We have to memorize the names and then the address, and then throw it out. So it was very interesting how cautious he was. We memorized the names and addresses. And I wanted to-- so, you know, I'd tear up the paper and throw it out the window of the moving train. And he said, no, no, they might find it and put it together. I said, what are you going to do with it. He said, I eat it. And he ate it. But nothing happened to him. Nothing got worse. And I managed to take over this group over the border. And on my way back, I was caught by two policemen-- border policemen. This was a forbidden district to go to the border. And, you know, I was walking on the highway. There was nobody there because it was a forbidden zone. And I was reading my bacteriology book, trying to prepare for an exam, when they stopped me. And they asked what I'm doing here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told him I was visiting a friend of the family in Putnok. But I missed the road and I am now coming back, because I knew that I was beyond Putnok. And he said, we will see about that. We are going to walk with you, to your friend. So you won't get lost again. And they were pushing their bicycle and we walked there. And this friend-- friend of my father, when he saw me with the two policemen, he immediately said, for heaven's sake, you know, what's happening with you? We were waiting for you all afternoon. So they let me go. He was very quick to realize what the situation is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there on, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I continued my studies without any interruption. I still met with my friend who was caught and was imprisoned by the Hungarian authorities. They let him out after a couple of years, and he had nobody in Budapest. His family immigrated, and he phoned me up whether I would be afraid to meet him. And I said, I would be afraid to met you, but I still would like to meet you. So we met in a neutral place and I filled him in with what has happened. For almost two years, he was in solitary, so he had no idea what's happened in the world. And the Israeli government managed to get him out. So after a few days, he left Hungary. And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] continued my studies at the University, which was again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an interesting field. Because the University of Budapest had a Communist Party organization which was, more and more, infiltrated by fascists. Former members of the Hungarian Fascist Party who immediately joined the Communist party had more and more influence. Interestingly enough, the first leadership of the Communist party at the University was Jewish. And this is understandable. I did not join the Hungarian Communist Party, but I didn't join it because I told that I have nothing to do with politics. But I certainly did not disagree with their principles. You probably know the saying that anybody who is 18 and not a communist does not have a heart. And anybody who is 30 and still a communist doesn't have a brain. I think it is true. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these fascists, who were opportunists, took over and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at one time-- this is what I told you earlier-- the Deputy Secretary of the Communist Party called me in and told me that, with a Zionist name like this, I cannot be a doctor in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what can I do? That's my name. So he said to change it or drop out. And I decided not to drop out. And so this is how I changed it. Which is really a shame because I don't think that there was ever a Norbert Kohn in the world, and I don't think there will be ever again, because these two names are so inappropriate together. And you chose the name Kerenyi instead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was there a reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My younger brother, who decided to join the Communist Party, who felt that he's still Hungarian, decided to change his name. And he changed his name to Kerenyi. And I disagreed with him, but I could not convince him not to do it. And then I had to do it. I decided at least I'd take the same name that he had, so it was not just without any reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me about an incident with a professor, who pointed out the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the name difference. You know, the first exam that I had after this name change in the first year was radiology. And the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Deputy Professor of Radiology was someone who was a former member of the Hungarian Fascist Party and was not allowed to rejoin the profession up until that year. But they gradually were allowed to rejoin, and they felt more and more secure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I went to have my radiology exam-- and this was on the first day of the exam-- according to the Hungarian system, the exams were oral and there were-- was an audience. It-- they were in a large classroom, and there were about a hundred people listening to this. And as I gave my index to him and the paper that was sent from the Dean's office to take the exam-- the permission to take the exam-- he immediately picked up the name difference. And he said that, how come, [INAUDIBLE], that your index says that you are Norbert Kohn and your paper from the Dean's office says that you are Norbert Kerenyi?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him, sir, it's very simple. Kohn is my maiden name. And this created a great deal of happiness amongst the audience, who all knew me. And most of them were friends, whether they were Jews or gentile. And it really embarrassed him instead of embarrassing me. He quickly let me pass with distinction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other incidents of antisemitism during this post-war period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many. You know, it's-- antisemitism did not die in Hungary with the fascist power. It's a very strong feature of Hungary. Maybe not as strong as in Poland, but a large percentage of the population are antisemitic. I think in most Catholic countries this is the case. And after the war, there were many incidents which was very difficult to-- to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cope with, because I discovered early that they are in a majority. I can't really take successful action. You know, sometimes, you know, one has, by coincidence, for example, indirectly, I have contributed to the death off my uncle's [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] former partner, who was instrumental to have him arrested. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But these things came as an accident rather than that I could do anything to-- to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] contribute to the people's-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to have any satisfaction. For example, sometimes I did. A classmate of my brother recognized me during the siege of Budapest. I was in a fascist uniform. He was a member of the Hungarian SS, also in fascist uniform. He recognized me, of course. We said hi to each other. And then he followed me to the place where I went in, and he demanded from the chief that I am a Jew who is trying to hide and he wanted to take me to the fascist headquarter. And he told him that he knows that because he organized it, and I am very important, that he should go and fight against the Russian on the front, much rather than interfere with designs that were decided by the party. So he left. But after the war, when we were lining up to a soccer game, somebody from the back said that, you should not try to get ahead of your way. And I looked back and it was him. So I managed to make a citizen's arrest. And he was arrested and tried for his war activities. Not just what he did against me. But these things were rare, and only occurred [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] immediately after the war. When they regained their balance, the antisemitism was there. And, you know, when you went to soccer games where there was less police control, it was always visible. And it's hard to judge whether this is a large number of people, a small number of people, but vocal. But certainly, antisemitism was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have a little time. I know you were in Hungary at the time of the revolution in 1956. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tell me a bit about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told that there's an interesting event, and it also has to do with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish people in Hungary. The revolution was a spontaneous one. And while the major demonstration was started as a sympathy demonstration towards the Poles by university students, by the time evening came, they were trying to have certain demands read in the Hungarian radio. Which was very similar to demands a hundred years prior to that, when there was another Hungarian revolution. And the radio was defended by members of the secret police. There were a couple hundred them there. And they did not want to read in the demands of the people who were demonstrating. And they tried to break in, and they started to shoot. And a few people died who were waiting there. What was surprising that nobody moved. But eventually, workers came from the light factory, which, in fact, was manufacturing sub-machine guns, and they attacked the radio. And they panicked, and they sent out the Hungarian army. And the army finished the Communist government in less than eight hours

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to-- if I could-- to ask about your experience at that time. You, yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Interestingly enough, I am in the group. You know, many people felt that, if the revolution would have won, it would have been sanctions against the Jews. And there were many antisemitic manifestations-- a certain group of the revolutionists. But on the other hand, our institute elected me be a member of revolutionary committee, and I was very close to happenings. And the antisemitic aspect of that revolution was certainly significant, but was not winning. The main trend was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Patriotic Hungarian. And my own experience was that I thought that we were not in danger. But once the revolution was defeated, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I was in danger. And this is one of the reasons why I had to leave so abruptly. And I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what eventually brought you to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I always wanted to leave Communist Hungary. But the revolution speeded things up because it gave the opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder if I could ask you some general questions about why you came forward to be interviewed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Several reasons. One that I always felt that these events should be preserved, and preserved from several points of view. Everybody see it differently. Everybody has a story to tell. But everybody, perhaps, has his own individual interpretation of the events. And I thought that the story that I want to tell should remain there. It was also attractive that someday my-- members of my own family, if they get interested, will [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] be listening to it, something that I could not make them to listen to in continuity. Because they instinctively are horrified by this and they don't really want to get into this. But perhaps later, if this is there, they will be interested. And anybody else who is interested. These are important events, and perhaps have this. And thirdly, I really wanted to mention here my older brother, who was killed during this war, and of course, could not be interviewed by anybody. But he also represented an aspect of our family, the most religious ones. And I was always concerned about his death, partly because of my major miscalculation. I didn't tell you that our survival-- our whole family's survival-- was organized by me at age 16. My parents were not able to cope, so they just sat down on a piece of luggage. My mother cried and told us to leave them because they had a long life already and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they won't be able to cope with this. And I managed to organize this. But in the case of my brother, I completely misjudged the situation. I let him go. I don't know whether or not he would have stayed if I would've wanted him to stay. But this was also a reason to-- to say this. And his religion was so strong, his trust in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] God's caring for him was so strong that, in my opinion, it may have contributed to his death. Because he was so law-abiding. And one of the last news that I heard about him from Buchenwald that he gave up a whole week of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bread ration for a prayers book, because, although he knew most of his prayers by heart, he wanted to be sure that he doesn't miss anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought that I will have an opportunity to-- other than the few names, because his name is in a few monuments in Hungary. But other than that, once we are gone, nobody will remember him. So this is a monument to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think it's a little bit more of a memory of him than what we would have otherwise. And this would be accessible to people and everybody can judge it his own way.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much. We're going to look at a few photographs that you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is my mother. Droth Rosa, her maiden name. My brother, whom I talked about, to her right. And me, to her left. It's easy to know that which one is me because I am the younger one. My brother is Gabor and my name is Norbert. And this is the three of us. I don't know what occasion this picture was taken, and I just received it yesterday from Hungary. My brother recovered it from somebody who had it, because we didn't have pictures left. So this is this picture. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my brother. It's another, better picture. There's not too many pictures left. In the last night, when the Russian liberated the area where we lived, our house, my father's store, and everything we owned was burned. And I always said that, during this whole ordeal, that I just would like to survive as I am, and I'm not interested in anything else. And this is exactly what has happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the Boy Scout group to which I also belonged, as I mentioned to you during the interview. And my brother, even I had difficulty to find. But he is on the very left of the picture. And it's interesting. This characterized him. That's the one. That's him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he in the first row, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's in the first row and in the very left of the picture. And he's the one who is not in uniform. Because the fact that he is so far outside of the center and no uniform is really-- characterizes him. He was rather absent-minded and probably left his uniform at home. And he never liked to be in the center, so he's on the side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No I am not. It is his age group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my daughter, Vivian, and this is on her engagement party. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I suppose this is all what I could say about this picture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is our middle child, Robert. And of course, he's much older now, but this was the only picture that I could get fast. He still looks similar to this. And right now he is with American Telephone and Telegraph Company. A financial analyst. This is my son, John. He's a lawyer now. This is a picture of my wife, Eva. Eva [? Genich, ?] her maiden name. And, of course, the other person is myself. A little bit younger than we are now.
